narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 46% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed right away. The key driver pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile—this stood out as the strongest negative signal, suggesting challenges in maintaining strong performance later in your studies. Counterbalancing that, your undergraduate GPA of 3.60 was a significant positive factor, well above the average of 3.21, helping pull the prediction toward success. Your LSAT score of 33 also worked in your favor, though it was below the average of about 37, providing moderate support for passing. Other details, like your middle family income bracket and planned full-time enrollment—which is the norm for over 90% of students—played smaller roles. Compared to typical applicants, your early Year 1 decile of 3 was below average too, but it wasn't as influential here. While your strong undergrad record shows real promise, the model weighed your later academic decile most heavily, indicating it sees that as the biggest risk for bar exam outcomes. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many with similar backgrounds overcome hurdles—but the system's focus on these patterns led to the no. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep programs, and reapplying to schools with more holistic reviews that might value your unique story. You could also target bar prep resources early or explore part-time options if that fits better. With targeted improvements, your solid GPA foundation positions you well for a stronger prediction next time. Keep pushing—law needs voices like yours.
